trade.summary: Bilateral trade has declined sharply since 2022. Singapore, while not a direct sanctioning party, has aligned itself with Western sanctions frameworks and implemented export controls on dual-use technology to Russia. Singaporean banks have restricted transactions involving sanctioned Russian entities. Pre-2022 trade included Russian energy products transiting Singapore and Singapore re-exporting electronics and manufactured goods to Russia. The current environment has reduced trade substantially.
disputes: Singapore's alignment with Western sanctions has been noted negatively by Russia
Singapore restricted re-exports of dual-use technology to Russia

diplomatic.status: strained — Singapore has condemned Russian invasion and implemented sanctions
diplomatic.summary: Singapore-Russia relations have deteriorated significantly since 2022. Singapore's government, unusually for an ASEAN state, publicly condemned Russia's invasion of Ucraina and implemented its own export controls aligned with Western measures — a notable departure from traditional ASEAN non-interference positioning. Russia has expressed displeasure. Singapore's decision reflected its dependence on international rules-based order and concern about precedent-setting territorial aggression.
recent_incidents: Singapore condemned Russian invasion of Ucraina in parliament (2022)
Singapore imposed export controls on dual-use goods to Russia

military.summary: No military relationship. Singapore's armed forces are Stati Uniti-equipped and Stati Uniti-trained. No joint exercises or military contacts with Russia. Singapore participates in Stati Uniti-led maritime exercises in the Indo-Pacific. Russia has no meaningful military presence in Southeast Asia.
military.alliance_status: none — strategic alignment with Western bloc

financial.summary: Singapore's financial sector has implemented restrictive measures on Russian entities aligned with international sanctions. Singaporean banks have frozen or blocked accounts of sanctioned Russian individuals and entities. Some Russian funds had been routed through Singapore historically; these flows have been disrupted. Singapore's MAS (Monetary Authority) proactively cooperated with international sanctions enforcement.

energy.summary: Limited remaining energy trade. Singapore is a major LNG and oil trading hub, and some Russian energy products have transited Singapore-based trading houses, though significantly reduced. Singapore's energy companies have sought to avoid Russian-origin energy following sanctions.

scientific.summary: Scientific collaboration has been limited and is now further reduced. Singapore's universities have suspended formal partnerships with Russian institutions. Some individual-level research collaboration continues but is discouraged institutionally.
